chain_name: Cool climate → high acidity in finished wine

steps.label: cause
steps.text: Cool growing environment (lower temperatures, high altitude, or high-latitude location)

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: Slow ripening delays sugar accumulation and preserves natural grape acids (malic and tartaric), reducing the rate at which malic acid is metabolised before harvest

steps.label: effect
steps.text: Wines retain higher acidity, show freshness and crispness, and typically have lower alcohol levels

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: Cool climates slow ripening, preserving acidity and producing wines with higher acidity, freshness, and lower alcohol levels.

starting_factor.description: Growing season temperatures are at the lower end of what allows full grape ripening.

intermediate_steps.description: Low temperatures slow the rate of photosynthesis and grape ripening.
Slower ripening means malic acid (which degrades enzymatically as grapes ripen) is retained at higher levels at harvest. Tartaric acid, being stable, remains constant regardless of ripening speed.
Lower sugar accumulation at harvest results in lower potential alcohol, meaning less dilution of acid perception in the finished wine.

final_outcome.description: Higher total acidity and lower pH in the finished wine, perceived as freshness and crispness on the palate.

sat_relevance: Use when justifying high acidity as a positive structural element in SAT quality assessment. High acidity in a cool-climate context is a feature, not a fault — link it to freshness, food-pairing versatility, and ageing potential.

examples.description: Cool Mosel valley temperatures result in high residual malic acid and characteristically high total acidity, providing structure and longevity even in wines with significant residual sugar.
Cool Champagne climate produces high-acidity, low-alcohol base wines — intentionally sought because secondary fermentation and dosage add sweetness that must be balanced by this natural acidity.

related_exam_questions: Explain how climate affects the acidity of a finished wine.
Why do cool-climate wines tend to have higher acidity than warm-climate wines from the same grape variety?
Describe the relationship between ripening rate and acid retention in grapes.
distinction_note: A Distinction answer explains the mechanism (malic acid degradation rate during ripening) not merely the correlation (cool climate = high acidity). It also notes that tartaric acid behaves differently from malic acid, and may mention diurnal variation as a nuancing factor.
